result:
At night, cities are cared for by people you rarely notice in daylight. Street sweepers draw wet commas along the curbs; a lone librarian in a lit window sorts a drift of paper into quiet order; the person who tends the greenhouse whispers to plants because grief needs somewhere to go. Maintenance is the soft grammar of existence, and most of us read it only when it fails. The hinge that does not squeal, the corner that does not gather dust, the train that arrives with all its parts confess a thousand small fidelities.

I once lived across from a bakery that started work at three. I learned the rhythm by scent before I knew the faces: proofing, baking, cooling, the faint percussion of trays. Some mornings, I’d wake to a thin veil of flour turning the dark into a low fog. It felt like weather invented by hands. When the first customers arrived, they thanked the day, not the bakers. I do this too. I thank the sunrise and forget the electricians who keep it from being mistaken for fire.

There is glamour in beginnings and endings, but the middle is where life asks for craft. A kept promise is maintenance. So is a friendship watered with check-in texts, a sidewalk cleared after snow, the deep breath you take before replying. I think of the world as a chorus held together by altos you can’t quite pick out. If you ever meet one, say thanks. Try to hum along.
